of the causes for sterility, though sterility is often associated
with normal capacity for coitus. In cases of sterility, sexual
functions are apparently normal, but the semen contains
either no sperms or too few. Absence .of sperms is
scientifically known as azoospermia while insufficiency of
sperms is called oligospermia. In most cases the cause is
supposed to lie in an earlier inflammation of the epididimys
of gonorrhoeal origion. This is, to say the least,
unwarranted because, when the father, grandfather and
great grandfather of the person concerned had never been
guilty of moral lapses and they had never suffered from
gonorrhoeal infection, how can the 'absence of sperm' in
the person concerned have

The Fifth House 219
gonorrhoeal origin? When science cannot explain things
with the aid of commonsense reasoning and ordinary logic
it resorts to unambiguous and unnatural causes. On the
